subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 2 Math, 24 students. You modelled perimeter and area for rectangles — P = 2(l + w) measured in cm, A = l × w measured in cm² — and set a series of irregular shape problems. Students are now 20 minutes in. Most have correct numerical answers, but the unit labels are wrong or missing, and the fastest students are already done and disengaged.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: Perimeter and area both correct with correct units. Finished early and is now doodling on the margin of his page.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Correct numerical answers for perimeter and area. Units wrong — has written cm² for perimeter and cm for area.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Perimeter correct with correct unit. Area answers look like guesses — different numbers each attempt, no working shown.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Both perimeter and area calculations wrong. Has confused the formulas — using l × w for perimeter and 2(l + w) for area.

ep1.situation: 20 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the pattern is mixed. Rajan has correct perimeter and area answers with correct units for all questions and is now doodling in the margin — he finished early and has nothing to do. Nur has correct numerical answers for both perimeter and area but has swapped the units: she has written cm² for perimeter and cm for area. The numbers are right but the labels are wrong. Wei Jie has the perimeter correct with the right unit, but his area answers vary across attempts — different numbers each time, no working shown. He appears to be guessing the area rather than calculating it. Siti has confused the formulas entirely — she is using l × w for perimeter and 2(l + w) for area, producing wrong answers for both. A quick scan of the class: 8 students are correct with correct units like Rajan. 10 students have correct numbers but wrong or missing units like Nur. 4 students are guessing area like Wei Jie. 2 students have formula confusion like Siti. You have about 20 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: Most students can calculate correctly but cannot correctly label their answers with units. The gap is not in arithmetic — it is in understanding what perimeter and area measure and why they use different units. The fastest students are disengaged and need extension.

choices.label: Two-step unit diagnostic
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a simple rectangle: length 5 cm, width 3 cm. Ask two questions in sequence: '(1) Calculate the perimeter and area. Show your working.' Then: '(2) Why does perimeter use cm but area uses cm²? Choose: (a) Because perimeter measures length around the edge. (b) Because area measures squares covering the surface. (c) Because the formula uses different operations. (d) I am not sure.' Ask students to write both answers on paper. Use the pattern to see who understands the unit distinction and who is applying units by memory rather than understanding.
choices.tradeoff: The two-step structure reveals both the calculation accuracy and the reasoning behind the unit choice, giving you whole-class evidence of where the unit confusion sits. But it halts the practice of students who are working productively and may feel like a test to students who are already correct. Rajan, who is already done, may disengage further if the diagnostic feels too easy.

choices.label: Extension proofs for fast finishers, unit prompts for middle
choices.elaboration: Distribute differentiated task cards without stopping the class. Fast finishers like Rajan receive an extension: 'Prove why perimeter uses cm and area uses cm². Draw a diagram to support your proof. Then: can you find a shape where the perimeter and area have the same numerical value? Explain why this happens.' Middle students like Nur receive a unit prompt card: 'Perimeter measures the ___ around the outside — it is a length, so the unit is ___. Area measures the ___ covering the surface — it is made of squares, so the unit is ___.' Struggling students like Siti receive a formula reference card with both formulas labelled.
choices.tradeoff: The extension task challenges fast finishers with genuine mathematical reasoning rather than more of the same. The unit prompt gives middle students a scaffold without doing the thinking for them. But the differentiated cards may feel stigmatising if students notice the different levels. Siti, who has formula confusion, needs more than a reference card — she needs to understand why the formulas are different.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: why do perimeter and area use different units?
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and assign shoulder partners. Ask them to discuss: 'Perimeter measures ___ because ___. Area measures ___ because ___. That is why they use different units.' The listener must ask: 'Can you prove it using the shape on the board?' Circulate and listen for pairs where the reasoning is genuine versus memorised. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with a clear conceptual explanation, one with a shallow or incorrect one — and run a brief IRF probe: 'What did you notice about these two explanations? Which reasoning is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk pushes students to articulate the conceptual distinction between perimeter and area, which may surface reasoning that the calculations alone do not reveal. But fast finishers like Rajan may dominate the discussion and give the answer before their partner has had time to think. Siti, who has formula confusion, may echo her partner without engaging with the underlying distinction.

A.consequence: The responses come in and the pattern is clear. 22 of 24 students calculated both perimeter and area correctly. But on the unit reasoning question, only 14 of 24 chose the correct options — 8 students chose option (a) for perimeter but option (c) for area, suggesting they understand perimeter as a length but are uncertain about why area uses cm². Nur got both calculations right but chose option (c) for both — she knows the units by memory but cannot explain why. Rajan got both right and chose options (a) and (b) with a clear explanation: 'Perimeter is a distance around the edge — one dimension, so cm. Area is a surface covered by squares — two dimensions multiplied, so cm².' Wei Jie got the perimeter right but guessed the area calculation — his answer varies. Siti got both calculations wrong and chose option (d) for both. The evidence is now precise: the majority error is unit labelling without conceptual understanding, and the formula confusion for Siti is separate and more fundamental. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: model the unit logic
choices.elaboration: Address the unit confusion directly with the whole class. Draw a 4 cm × 3 cm rectangle on the board. Explain perimeter: 'Perimeter is the distance around the edge — I am measuring a length, one dimension, so the unit is cm.' Then explain area: 'Area is the number of 1 cm × 1 cm squares that cover the surface — I am multiplying two lengths together, so the unit is cm × cm = cm².' Draw the squares inside the rectangle to make this visual. Ask all students to re-label their answers with the correct units and write one sentence explaining why each unit is correct.
choices.tradeoff: The reteach addresses the unit confusion with a clear visual explanation and will reach the 10 students who are labelling by memory rather than understanding. But it reteaches students like Rajan who already understand, which under-challenges them. Siti, who has formula confusion, needs a different intervention — the unit reteach does not address her more fundamental error.

choices.label: AQS peer feedback on unit labels
choices.elaboration: Use the diagnostic evidence to form pairs: students who got both units right (like Rajan) are paired with students who swapped the units (like Nur) or guessed (like Wei Jie). The correct student gives AQS feedback on their partner's working: Affirm the correct calculation ('Your perimeter calculation is right — 16 cm'), Question the unit ('Why did you write cm² for perimeter? What is perimeter measuring?'), Suggest the correction ('Perimeter measures a length around the edge — one dimension — so the unit is cm, not cm²'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise in the room. Nur, who has correct calculations but wrong units, may benefit from having the unit distinction explained by a peer in conversational terms. But Siti has formula confusion — her partner will need to address a more fundamental error than unit labelling, and the AQS structure may not be sufficient. Rajan is giving rather than receiving.

B.consequence: The differentiated cards go out and students begin working. Rajan receives the extension card and immediately engages — he draws a diagram showing a 1 cm × 1 cm square and writes: 'Perimeter = 4 × 1 = 4 cm (one dimension). Area = 1 × 1 = 1 cm² (two dimensions multiplied).' He then works on the equal-value problem and finds that a 4 × 4 square has P = 16 cm and A = 16 cm² — same number, different units. He writes: 'The numerical values are equal but the units are different — they measure different things.' Nur receives the unit prompt card and fills in the blanks correctly: 'Perimeter measures the distance around the outside — it is a length, so the unit is cm. Area measures the surface covered — it is made of squares, so the unit is cm².' She re-labels her answers correctly. Wei Jie receives the unit prompt card and fills in the blanks, but when you ask him to explain area in his own words, he reads from the card rather than paraphrasing. Siti receives the formula reference card but continues to use the wrong formula — she is not reading the card carefully. The scaffolds have given every student an entry point, but the depth of engagement varies. 19 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF probe: why do perimeter and area use different units?
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run an IRF chain. Ask: 'Rajan, from your extension work — why does perimeter use cm but area uses cm²?' (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you build on that? What does cm² actually represent?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'Do you agree? Can you give an example that proves it?' (Feedback). Use the chain to build a complete, class-constructed explanation of the unit distinction. Write the final explanation on the board.
choices.tradeoff: The IRF probe leverages Rajan's extension work and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. It gives the middle group a clear, class-built explanation of the unit distinction. But it puts students on the spot and may overwhelm Siti, who is still confused about the formulas. The probe serves the middle group well but does not address Siti's more fundamental formula confusion.

choices.label: Self-check application: new shape, units and logic
choices.elaboration: Give a new irregular shape problem. Ask students to calculate perimeter and area, label with correct units, and then write a self-check: 'Perimeter uses ___ because ___. Area uses ___ because ___. I check my units by ___.' Give 4 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuinely internalised the unit distinction and who was dependent on the prompt card.
choices.tradeoff: The self-check application tests whether the unit distinction has been internalised or just read from the card. Rajan and Nur are likely ready — Rajan because he proved the distinction, Nur because she filled in the prompt correctly. But Wei Jie, who was reading from the card rather than paraphrasing, may not be ready. Siti, who has formula confusion, will produce wrong calculations regardless of unit understanding.

C.consequence: The pairs begin talking and the room gets louder. Rajan explains to his partner with precision: 'Perimeter measures the distance around the outside — it is one-dimensional, like a line, so the unit is cm. Area measures the surface covered — it is two-dimensional, like squares tiled on the floor, so the unit is cm × cm = cm².' His partner asks: 'Can you prove it using the shape?' Rajan draws a diagram. Nur says to her partner: 'Perimeter is around the outside.' Her partner asks: 'Why cm and not cm²?' Nur says: 'Because it is not squares.' Her reasoning is correct but shallow — she cannot articulate the one-dimensional versus two-dimensional distinction. Wei Jie says to his partner: 'Are they the same unit?' His partner says: 'No, one is cm and one is cm².' Wei Jie nods but does not ask why. Siti echoes her partner's explanation without engaging. You circulate and hear the pattern: 6 pairs have deep conceptual explanations with the dimensional reasoning. 10 pairs are correct but shallow — they know the units but cannot explain why. 8 pairs are echoing or uncertain. The talk has revealed some reasoning but has not deepened the unit understanding for most students. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS gallery critique on unit explanations
choices.elaboration: Ask each pair to write their unit explanation on a sticky note: 'Perimeter uses cm because ___. Area uses cm² because ___.' Post four notes on the board: one with the dimensional reasoning, one with 'because it is not squares', one with 'because the formula is different', one with no explanation. Run a whole-class AQS session on the shallow note: Affirm what they attempted ('They identified the correct units'), Question what is missing ('Why is perimeter not squares? What is it measuring?'), Suggest how to deepen it ('Think about dimensions — perimeter is one-dimensional like a line, area is two-dimensional like a surface'). Ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between shallow and deep unit reasoning visible to the whole class. It gives the shallow pairs a concrete model of what dimensional reasoning looks like. But the pairs whose notes are displayed may feel exposed. Siti's pair will likely produce a shallow or incorrect note, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who are still confused about the formulas.

choices.label: Solo unit proof: write the dimensional reasoning
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair talk and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'Prove why perimeter uses cm and area uses cm². Your proof must include: (1) What each measurement measures. (2) Why the unit follows from what it measures. (3) A diagram or example to support your proof.' Give 4 minutes, then collect the responses.
choices.tradeoff: Solo written proofs give you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's conceptual understanding — uncontaminated by pair talk. Rajan's proof will show deep dimensional reasoning. But the move to individual work kills the emerging talk momentum. Siti, who was echoing her partner, will likely produce a blank or copied response. Wei Jie, who was uncertain about the unit distinction, will likely produce a shallow proof.

A1.outcome: You draw the rectangle on the board and explain the unit logic with the visual of squares inside. The room goes quiet as students watch. Nur looks at the diagram and immediately re-labels her answers: she crosses out cm² for perimeter and writes cm, and crosses out cm for area and writes cm². She says quietly: 'Oh — I had them backwards.' Wei Jie follows the explanation and re-labels his perimeter answer correctly. He still has not calculated area — the reteach has addressed the unit but not his guessing of the area value. Rajan watches the reteach with visible disengagement — he already understood this and the whole-class explanation does not challenge him. He has returned to his extension doodling. Siti copies the board diagram and re-labels her answers, but her calculations are still wrong — she is still using the wrong formulas. The unit reteach has not addressed her formula confusion. On a follow-up check, 23 of 24 students now have correct unit labels, up from 14. The unit confusion has been addressed for almost everyone. But Wei Jie still has not calculated area correctly, Siti still has formula confusion, and Rajan is under-challenged. 15 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with visual explanation is efficient and reaches the majority of students who had unit confusion. It is process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you explained what the units measure and why. Nur made a clear gain. But the reteach treats the class as uniform. Rajan is under-challenged. Siti's formula confusion is a more fundamental error that the unit reteach does not address.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose whole-class reteach over peer feedback? What were you prioritising — efficiency, clarity, or equity?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Most students now have correct units, but Rajan is disengaged, Wei Jie still cannot calculate area, and Siti still has formula confusion. What does this tell you about the difference between fixing a labelling error and building conceptual understanding?

How do you design a lesson so that fast finishers are challenged throughout, not just at the end when you notice they are done?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's unit labels. Rajan looks at Nur's page and begins: 'Your perimeter calculation is right — 16 cm. But you have written cm² for perimeter. Perimeter measures the distance around the edge — it is one-dimensional, like a line, so the unit is cm, not cm².' Nur listens, nods, and re-labels her answer. She says: 'Oh — I had them swapped. Area is the squares, so that is cm².' Rajan nods. Wei Jie is paired with a student who has correct units. His partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your perimeter is right. But your area answers are different each time — are you calculating or guessing?' Wei Jie admits he was guessing. His partner explains the formula: 'Area is length times width — for this shape, you need to split it into rectangles first.' Wei Jie attempts the calculation and gets it right. Siti is paired with a student who has correct calculations and units. Her partner gives AQS feedback but quickly realises Siti has the formulas swapped — the partner explains both formulas from scratch. Siti re-does her calculations with the correct formulas and gets them right. On a follow-up check, 20 of 24 students now have correct calculations and units, up from 14. The peer feedback has activated genuine reasoning for Nur, Wei Jie, and Siti. But Rajan is giving rather than receiving. 15 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activated genuine reasoning for three students. Nur corrected her unit labels, Wei Jie stopped guessing and calculated correctly, and Siti corrected her formula confusion through peer explanation. But Rajan is under-challenged — giving feedback is not the same as being challenged with new content. The quality of peer explanation also varied: Siti's partner had to explain both formulas from scratch, which went beyond the AQS structure.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class reteach? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Three students made genuine gains, but Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in a fast-finisher class?

How do you design extension tasks that genuinely challenge fast finishers — not just more of the same, but deeper mathematical reasoning?

B1.outcome: You pause the class and run the IRF chain. You ask Rajan: 'From your extension work — why does perimeter use cm but area uses cm²?' Rajan responds: 'Perimeter is one-dimensional — it measures a length around the edge, so the unit is cm. Area is two-dimensional — it measures a surface covered by squares, so the unit is cm × cm = cm².' You ask a second student to build on this: 'So cm² means centimetre squared — it is a square with sides of 1 cm, and area counts how many of those squares fit.' A third student evaluates: 'Yes — and that is why you multiply length by width for area, because you are counting squares in rows and columns.' The IRF chain produces a complete, class-constructed explanation. Nur listens carefully and re-labels her answers. Wei Jie follows the chain and nods. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask her 'Does that make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. Her calculations are still wrong — the unit explanation has not addressed her formula confusion. On a follow-up check, 20 of 24 students can now explain the unit distinction, up from 8. 15 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The IRF probe leverages Rajan's extension work and builds a shared explanation that is richer and more precise than a teacher-led reteach. It gives the middle group a clear, class-built explanation of the dimensional reasoning. Rajan is genuinely engaged — the probe has given him a platform for his extension thinking. But Siti's formula confusion is not addressed by the unit explanation, and she remains stuck on a more fundamental error.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run an IRF probe using Rajan's extension work? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced strong dimensional reasoning from the middle group, but Siti's formula confusion remains unaddressed. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class discussion when students have different types of errors?

How do you design extension tasks so that fast finishers contribute to the class's learning — not just their own?

B2.outcome: You give the new irregular shape problem and ask students to calculate, label, and self-check. Rajan completes it quickly with full working and a detailed self-check: 'Perimeter uses cm because it measures a one-dimensional length around the edge. Area uses cm² because it measures a two-dimensional surface — squares of 1 cm × 1 cm. I check my units by asking: am I measuring a length or a surface?' His response shows deep understanding and metacognitive awareness. Nur calculates correctly and labels with correct units. Her self-check says: 'Perimeter uses cm because it is a length. Area uses cm² because it is squares. I check by looking at the formula.' She has internalised the unit distinction from the prompt card. Wei Jie calculates perimeter correctly but guesses area again — his self-check says: 'I am not sure how to calculate area for this shape.' The scaffold removal has revealed that he was reading the unit prompt without understanding how to calculate area for irregular shapes. Siti calculates both incorrectly — she is still using the wrong formulas. Her self-check is blank. 15 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: The self-check application tests genuine internalisation honestly. Rajan and Nur demonstrated real understanding — they could calculate, label, and explain the reasoning. But Wei Jie's area calculation gap is now visible — he understood the unit distinction but cannot calculate area for irregular shapes. Siti's formula confusion remains unaddressed. You have clean evidence of two different types of gaps — but limited time to address both.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to test transfer with a self-check application? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur succeeded, but Wei Jie cannot calculate area for irregular shapes and Siti still has formula confusion. What does this tell you about the difference between unit understanding and calculation ability?

How do you design a lesson that addresses both the conceptual gap (units) and the procedural gap (area calculation for irregular shapes) without losing the fast finishers?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the shallow note: 'Perimeter uses cm because it is not squares.' Several students affirm: 'They identified the correct unit.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand: 'Why is it not squares? What is perimeter measuring?' You write: 'Question: What does perimeter measure? Why is it one-dimensional?' A third student suggests: 'Think about dimensions — perimeter is like a line around the edge, area is like a surface covered by squares.' You write: 'Suggest: Perimeter = one dimension (length). Area = two dimensions (length × width).' The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes. Several shallow pairs add the dimensional reasoning to their notes. Wei Jie's pair revises their note to include 'one-dimensional' and 'two-dimensional.' Siti's pair produces a revised note that says 'perimeter is a length, area is squares' — correct but still shallow. When you ask Siti to demonstrate on a new question, she calculates with the wrong formula. 15 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique deepened the unit reasoning for several pairs and gave the shallow pairs a concrete model of dimensional thinking. Wei Jie's pair made a genuine gain. But Siti's formula confusion is not addressed by the unit discussion — she can now articulate the unit distinction but still cannot calculate correctly. The AQS process served the middle group but did not address the most fundamental error in the room.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Several pairs deepened their unit reasoning, but Siti's formula confusion remains unaddressed. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class feedback when students have different types of errors?

How do you design Accountable Talk tasks so that the conceptual reasoning is built through the discussion rather than just confirmed by the feedback?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their solo unit proofs. Rajan writes a detailed proof with a diagram: 'Perimeter measures the distance around the outside — it is one-dimensional, like a line. The unit is cm because we are measuring a single length. Area measures the surface covered — it is two-dimensional, like squares tiled on the floor. The unit is cm² because we are multiplying two lengths: cm × cm = cm².' His proof is complete and precise. Nur writes: 'Perimeter uses cm because it measures a length around the edge. Area uses cm² because it measures squares covering the surface.' Her proof is correct and clear. Wei Jie writes: 'Perimeter uses cm because it is a length. Area uses cm².' He stops — he cannot explain why area uses cm². His proof is incomplete. Siti writes: 'Perimeter uses cm. Area uses cm².' No explanation. Her calculations on the page are still wrong — she has the units right now but the formulas wrong. The solo proofs have given you honest individual evidence. Rajan and Nur have genuine conceptual understanding; Wei Jie has the labels but not the reasoning; Siti has the labels but not the formulas. 15 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Solo written proofs give you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's conceptual understanding. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine dimensional reasoning. But the move to individual work has stopped the emerging talk. Wei Jie's incomplete proof reveals he has the unit labels but not the dimensional reasoning. Siti's proof reveals she has the labels but still has formula confusion — the pair talk gave her the correct unit labels but did not address her more fundamental error.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo written proofs? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie has labels without reasoning and Siti has labels without correct formulas. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine conceptual understanding versus surface-level label correction?

How do you design a lesson that addresses both the conceptual gap (unit reasoning) and the procedural gap (formula confusion) for students like Siti?
